title: Mint sauce
subtitle: Fresh mint, vinegar, and a little sugar — the lamb companion
excerpt: Sharp, fragrant, and unmistakably British. Made in minutes from fresh mint leaves, malt vinegar, and a small amount of sugar. The only thing worth serving alongside a roast leg of lamb.
sourceNotes: Classic British condiment documented in Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — 'Mint Sauce for Roast Lamb', including the malt vinegar and sugar base. The proportions have changed little since.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 hours ahead and leave at room temperature. The mint will darken slightly but the flavour improves as it steeps.

content.text: Mint sauce takes about five minutes and a handful of garden mint. What it gives back is disproportionate: the cool, sharp contrast cuts straight through the richness of roast lamb. The ratio is not fixed: some cooks prefer more vinegar, some prefer less sugar, and the mint-to-liquid proportion varies by season as the leaves change character. Start with the recipe below and adjust to your mint.

Use a good bunch of fresh spearmint — garden mint if you grow it, supermarket mint if you don't. Do not use dried. The malt vinegar is traditional and correct; white wine vinegar produces a paler, slightly more refined result for those who prefer it.

Strip the leaves from the stalks and chop finely. Sprinkle {{granulated-sugar}} of granulated sugar over the chopped mint and work it in with the flat of the knife. The sugar draws out the mint's juices and softens the leaves slightly.

Transfer the mint and sugar to a small bowl. Pour over {{water}} of just-boiled water to help dissolve the sugar, then add {{malt-vinegar}} of malt vinegar. Stir and leave to steep for at least 10 minutes before serving. Taste and adjust — more vinegar for sharpness, a pinch more sugar if the mint is particularly pungent.

items.cause: Mint is mild or the vinegar proportion is high
items.fix: Add another pinch of sugar and stir. Taste again after a minute.

items.cause: Sugar measured generously or mint is a particularly sweet variety
items.fix: Stir in a splash more malt vinegar until the sharpness returns.

items.cause: Made several hours ahead; oxidation is normal
items.fix: The flavour is unaffected. If appearance matters, make it within 30 minutes of serving.

items.cause: Too much water relative to mint
items.fix: Strain off some of the liquid, or add more chopped mint. The sauce should be thick enough to spoon, not a thin liquid.

content.text: Mint sauce with roast lamb is one of the most persistent pairings in British cooking. Beeton's 1861 recipe is recognisably the same dish made today: mint leaves, sugar, vinegar. The French mint-and-lamb tradition reaches instead for redcurrant jelly and nothing else; the British answer is this sauce, which divides opinion abroad but remains unquestioned at the Sunday table. It works as well cold the next day, spooned over sliced leftover lamb with a piece of bread.
